requiredExam.label: Observe work of breathing
requiredExam.action: Document tachypnea, labored breathing, accessory muscle use, speech limitation, or apparent respiratory distress.
requiredExam.technique: Observe the patient at rest before auscultation; note respiratory effort, accessory muscle use, ability to speak, posture, and whether oxygen support is being used.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever workup because pneumonia, respiratory viral illness, shock, and hypoxemic respiratory failure can present with fever.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory severity: tachypnea, increased work of breathing, speech limitation, or worsening oxygen need.
requiredExam.management_change: Increased work of breathing changes oxygen strategy, chest imaging urgency, respiratory support, ED or ICU escalation, and reassessment frequency.
requiredExam.limitations: Work of breathing may be subtle in older adults, immunocompromised patients, neuromuscular disease, obesity, sedation, or early respiratory failure; pair with respiratory rate and oxygenation.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate posterior lung fields
requiredExam.action: Listen for focal crackles, wheeze, rhonchi, diminished sounds, or asymmetry suggesting pneumonia or another pulmonary source.
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate symmetric posterior lung fields from apices to bases when feasible, comparing side to side; include lateral or anterior fields if positioning limits posterior exam.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever workup unless clinically unsafe; prioritize when cough, sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, aspiration risk, or no clear source is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory fever source: pneumonia, aspiration, bronchospasm, effusion, edema, or asymmetric ventilation.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal lung sounds change chest imaging review, respiratory testing, oxygen/support, antimicrobial framing, bronchodilator treatment, isolation, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Normal auscultation does not exclude pneumonia or early lower respiratory infection; body habitus, poor inspiratory effort, ambient noise, positioning, and prior lung disease can reduce reliability.

conditionalExam.limitations: CVA tenderness is neither required nor sufficient for pyelonephritis or obstruction; body habitus, musculoskeletal pain, analgesia, and patient tolerance affect reliability.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate abdomen
conditionalExam.action: Look for focal tenderness, guarding, rebound-type concern, distension, or hepatobiliary pattern when abdominal or GI symptoms accompany fever.
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect first, then palpate all quadrants gently before deeper palpation, watching the patient's face and comparing tenderness by location; avoid provoking severe pain unnecessarily.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, distension, GI bleeding, peritonitis concern, or unclear abdominal source.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Abdominal fever source: peritonitis, appendicitis, biliary infection, colitis, obstruction, intra-abdominal abscess, or abdominal source-control concern.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abdominal tenderness or guarding changes imaging, surgical or source-control urgency, stool/infectious testing, antimicrobial framing, and escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Abdominal exam can be muted in older, immunocompromised, sedated, obese, pregnant, or analgesic-treated patients; normal exam does not exclude serious intra-abdominal infection.

source.source_section: abdominal pain and localizing fever clues

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Test neck stiffness
conditionalExam.action: Assess for meningismus when fever has severe headache, photophobia, confusion, seizure, rash, or CNS infection concern.
conditionalExam.technique: If safe and tolerated, ask about neck pain and gently assess passive neck flexion or obvious resistance; do not force movement when trauma, instability, or severe pain is present.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has CNS, meningeal, severe headache, photophobia, altered mental status, seizure, or petechial/purpuric rash features.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Possible meningitis, encephalitis, or CNS infection danger pattern.
conditionalExam.management_change: Neck stiffness with fever changes ED escalation, empiric antimicrobial urgency, isolation, blood cultures, neuroimaging decision-making, and lumbar puncture planning.
conditionalExam.limitations: Absence of meningismus does not exclude meningitis, especially in older, immunocompromised, sedated, or early presentations; avoid forced movement if trauma or instability is possible.

source.source_section: neurologic and meningeal red flags in fever

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Percuss posterior lung fields
conditionalExam.action: Add percussion when lung sounds or symptoms suggest focal consolidation, effusion, or asymmetric ventilation.
conditionalExam.technique: Percuss symmetric posterior lung fields side to side, comparing resonance and dullness from upper to lower fields as positioning allows.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add after auscultation when focal decreased sounds, pleuritic pain, dyspnea, effusion concern, or asymmetric pulmonary findings are present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Pulmonary consolidation, pleural effusion, hyperinflation, or asymmetric ventilation clue.
conditionalExam.management_change: Dullness or asymmetry supports chest imaging review, pneumonia or effusion framing, respiratory support reassessment, and escalation when paired with symptoms and auscultation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Percussion has limited reliability in noisy rooms, obesity, poor positioning, small effusions, or subtle consolidation; use as an add-on rather than a replacement for imaging when pneumonia is suspected.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect painful joint
conditionalExam.action: Inspect the symptomatic joint for swelling, erythema, warmth, deformity, and ability to move when fever has hot joint or focal musculoskeletal symptoms.
conditionalExam.technique: Compare the symptomatic joint with the opposite side when possible; document swelling, erythema, warmth by touch if safe, range limitation, and whether pain prevents movement.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has hot joint, focal bone pain, severe back pain, injection drug use, bacteremia risk, or musculoskeletal source concern.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Joint, bone, spine, or soft-tissue source such as septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess, or focal musculoskeletal infection concern.
conditionalExam.management_change: A hot swollen joint or severe focal spine/bone finding changes aspiration, imaging, blood cultures, antimicrobial timing, and orthopedic or spine escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Inspection alone cannot exclude septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, or epidural abscess; pain medication, immunosuppression, and limited access can blunt findings.

initialTests.label: Sepsis severity labs and reference thresholds
initialTests.action: Consider CBC with differential, CMP or creatinine, glucose when indicated, lactate when sepsis or hypoperfusion is possible, and review prior cultures or recent antimicrobials.
initialTests.rationale: Objective severity data is needed when fever may represent sepsis; lactate >2 mmol/L, lactate >=4 mmol/L, hypotension, or new organ dysfunction changes urgency and monitoring.

initialTests.label: Source-directed infection studies

root.description: Activate when adult patient context includes fever, suspected infection, systemic inflammatory symptoms, sepsis concern, shock physiology, or clinician-selected fever/sepsis workup.

endpoint_missing_context.description: Use this branch when the data needed to decide infection likelihood, shock physiology, source, or disposition cannot be extracted.

fever_infection_sepsis_initial_assessmen.description: Initial fever/infection evaluation requires source symptoms, complete vital signs, mental status, perfusion, oxygenation, organ dysfunction clues, cultures/lactate when sepsis is possible, medication/allergy risks, and host modifiers.

criteria_options.label: Acutely ill adult: screen with NEWS/NEWS2/MEWS/SIRS rather than qSOFA alone; sepsis is a clinical diagnosis, not a single biomarker rule-out
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS, or SIRS over qSOFA as a single sepsis screening tool in hospital; sepsis is a clinical diagnosis and should not be ruled in or ruled out with one biomarker/test.

criteria_options.label: Possible/probable/definite sepsis or shock: collect blood cultures as soon as possible, ideally before antimicrobials, and measure lactate
criteria_options.criteria_text: For adults with possible, probable, or definite sepsis or septic shock, SSC recommends blood cultures as soon as possible and ideally before antimicrobials, and suggests measuring blood lactate.

criteria_options.label: Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock: at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in first 3 hours with frequent reassessment
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours for adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock; use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal weight when BMI >30 kg/m2 and reassess frequently.

criteria_options.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
criteria_options.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.
criteria_options.source_section: Vasopressor administration and mean arterial pressure

criteria_options.label: Antimicrobials: septic shock or probable/definite sepsis within 1 hour; possible sepsis without shock rapid investigation then antibiotics within 3 hours if concern persists
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends antimicrobials immediately, ideally within 1 hour, for septic shock and probable/definite sepsis without shock; possible sepsis without shock should receive rapid investigation and antibiotics within 3 hours if infection concern persists, while low-likelihood infection without shock can defer antimicrobials with close monitoring.

criteria_options.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

criteria_options.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests ICU admission within 6 hours when ICU admission is required, SpO2 practice targets commonly 90-96% in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, and HFNC for sepsis-associated hypoxemic respiratory failure with PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235.
criteria_options.source_section: ICU admission, oxygen targets, respiratory support

criteria_options.label: Sepsis-associated ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau pressure <=30 cm H2O, prone ventilation >12 hours/day for moderate-severe ARDS
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

criteria_options.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests serial lactate to guide resuscitation in elevated lactate or shock, recommends antimicrobial de-escalation with confirmed microbiology/susceptibility, suggests de-escalation when final cultures identify no pathogen, and advises discontinuing empiric therapy when an alternative diagnosis is demonstrated or strongly suspected.

criteria_options.label: Survivor discharge: written/verbal sepsis diagnosis and treatment summary; follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional impairments
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 good-practice statements call for shared discharge planning, written/verbal summary of sepsis diagnosis/treatments/common impairments, and follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional problems after sepsis hospitalization.

criteria_options.label: Pregnancy/postpartum sepsis: maternal sepsis pathway and obstetric source/fetal considerations require clinician review
criteria_options.criteria_text: Pregnancy or postpartum status changes fever/sepsis evaluation, antimicrobial selection, source control, fetal considerations, and disposition; use maternal sepsis guidance and obstetric consultation.

criteria_options.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
criteria_options.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.
criteria_options.source_section: Vasopressor administration and mean arterial pressure

criteria_options.label: Antimicrobials: septic shock or probable/definite sepsis within 1 hour; possible sepsis without shock rapid investigation then antibiotics within 3 hours if concern persists
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends antimicrobials immediately, ideally within 1 hour, for septic shock and probable/definite sepsis without shock; possible sepsis without shock should receive rapid investigation and antibiotics within 3 hours if infection concern persists, while low-likelihood infection without shock can defer antimicrobials with close monitoring.

criteria_options.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

criteria_options.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests ICU admission within 6 hours when ICU admission is required, SpO2 practice targets commonly 90-96% in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, and HFNC for sepsis-associated hypoxemic respiratory failure with PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235.
criteria_options.source_section: ICU admission, oxygen targets, respiratory support

criteria_options.label: Sepsis-associated ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau pressure <=30 cm H2O, prone ventilation >12 hours/day for moderate-severe ARDS
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

criteria_options.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests serial lactate to guide resuscitation in elevated lactate or shock, recommends antimicrobial de-escalation with confirmed microbiology/susceptibility, suggests de-escalation when final cultures identify no pathogen, and advises discontinuing empiric therapy when an alternative diagnosis is demonstrated or strongly suspected.

criteria_options.label: Survivor discharge: written/verbal sepsis diagnosis and treatment summary; follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional impairments

criteria_options.label: Possible/probable/definite sepsis or shock: collect blood cultures as soon as possible, ideally before antimicrobials, and measure lactate
criteria_options.criteria_text: For adults with possible, probable, or definite sepsis or septic shock, SSC recommends blood cultures as soon as possible and ideally before antimicrobials, and suggests measuring blood lactate.

criteria_options.label: Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock: at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in first 3 hours with frequent reassessment
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours for adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock; use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal weight when BMI >30 kg/m2 and reassess frequently.

criteria_options.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
criteria_options.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.
criteria_options.source_section: Vasopressor administration and mean arterial pressure

criteria_options.label: Antimicrobials: septic shock or probable/definite sepsis within 1 hour; possible sepsis without shock rapid investigation then antibiotics within 3 hours if concern persists

criteria_options.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

criteria_options.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests ICU admission within 6 hours when ICU admission is required, SpO2 practice targets commonly 90-96% in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, and HFNC for sepsis-associated hypoxemic respiratory failure with PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235.

criteria_options.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected

children.action: Begin immediate sepsis resuscitation: obtain cultures/lactate without delaying therapy, give broad-spectrum antimicrobials ideally within 1 hour, administer at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours when hypoperfusion/shock is present, reassess fluid tolerance frequently, start norepinephrine/peripheral vasopressors if hypotension persists, target MAP 65 mm Hg (or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65), evaluate source control, and arrange monitored/ICU care within 6 hours when ICU admission is required.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65

guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS, or SIRS over qSOFA as a single sepsis screening tool in hospital; sepsis is a clinical diagnosis and should not be ruled in or ruled out with one biomarker/test.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Possible/probable/definite sepsis or shock: collect blood cultures as soon as possible, ideally before antimicrobials, and measure lactate
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: For adults with possible, probable, or definite sepsis or septic shock, SSC recommends blood cultures as soon as possible and ideally before antimicrobials, and suggests measuring blood lactate.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock: at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in first 3 hours with frequent reassessment
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours for adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock; use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal weight when BMI >30 kg/m2 and reassess frequently.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Vasopressor administration and mean arterial pressure

guideline_cutoffs.label: Antimicrobials: septic shock or probable/definite sepsis within 1 hour; possible sepsis without shock rapid investigation then antibiotics within 3 hours if concern persists
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends antimicrobials immediately, ideally within 1 hour, for septic shock and probable/definite sepsis without shock; possible sepsis without shock should receive rapid investigation and antibiotics within 3 hours if infection concern persists, while low-likelihood infection without shock can defer antimicrobials with close monitoring.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

guideline_cutoffs.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests ICU admission within 6 hours when ICU admission is required, SpO2 practice targets commonly 90-96% in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, and HFNC for sepsis-associated hypoxemic respiratory failure with PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: ICU admission, oxygen targets, respiratory support

guideline_cutoffs.label: Sepsis-associated ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau pressure <=30 cm H2O, prone ventilation >12 hours/day for moderate-severe ARDS
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests serial lactate to guide resuscitation in elevated lactate or shock, recommends antimicrobial de-escalation with confirmed microbiology/susceptibility, suggests de-escalation when final cultures identify no pathogen, and advises discontinuing empiric therapy when an alternative diagnosis is demonstrated or strongly suspected.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Survivor discharge: written/verbal sepsis diagnosis and treatment summary; follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional impairments
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 good-practice statements call for shared discharge planning, written/verbal summary of sepsis diagnosis/treatments/common impairments, and follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional problems after sepsis hospitalization.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Pregnancy/postpartum sepsis: maternal sepsis pathway and obstetric source/fetal considerations require clinician review
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Pregnancy or postpartum status changes fever/sepsis evaluation, antimicrobial selection, source control, fetal considerations, and disposition; use maternal sepsis guidance and obstetric consultation.

criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours for adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock; use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal weight when BMI >30 kg/m2 and reassess frequently.

criteria_options.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
criteria_options.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.

criteria_options.label: Antimicrobials: septic shock or probable/definite sepsis within 1 hour; possible sepsis without shock rapid investigation then antibiotics within 3 hours if concern persists

criteria_options.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

criteria_options.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235

criteria_options.label: Sepsis-associated ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau pressure <=30 cm H2O, prone ventilation >12 hours/day for moderate-severe ARDS
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

criteria_options.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected

criteria_options.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235

criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

criteria_options.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected

guideline_cutoffs.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65

guideline_cutoffs.label: Source control: evaluate emergent source-control diagnoses and perform early source control ideally within 6 hours when required
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 states adults with sepsis or shock should be rapidly evaluated for source-control diagnoses and suggests early source control, ideally within 6 hours of sepsis or shock diagnosis requiring source control.

guideline_cutoffs.label: ICU/respiratory escalation: ICU admission within 6 hours when required; oxygen target usually SpO2 90-96%; HFNC if PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests ICU admission within 6 hours when ICU admission is required, SpO2 practice targets commonly 90-96% in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, and HFNC for sepsis-associated hypoxemic respiratory failure with PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <235.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: ICU admission, oxygen targets, respiratory support

guideline_cutoffs.label: Sepsis-associated ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau pressure <=30 cm H2O, prone ventilation >12 hours/day for moderate-severe ARDS
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg in sepsis-associated ARDS, plateau pressure upper limit 30 cm H2O, and prone ventilation for greater than 12 hours daily in moderate-severe ARDS.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Monitoring/de-escalation: use serial lactate when lactate elevated or shock; de-escalate when cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow; stop empiric therapy if alternate diagnosis strongly suspected
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests serial lactate to guide resuscitation in elevated lactate or shock, recommends antimicrobial de-escalation with confirmed microbiology/susceptibility, suggests de-escalation when final cultures identify no pathogen, and advises discontinuing empiric therapy when an alternative diagnosis is demonstrated or strongly suspected.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Survivor discharge: written/verbal sepsis diagnosis and treatment summary; follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional impairments
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 good-practice statements call for shared discharge planning, written/verbal summary of sepsis diagnosis/treatments/common impairments, and follow-up for new physical, cognitive, or emotional problems after sepsis hospitalization.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Pregnancy/postpartum sepsis: maternal sepsis pathway and obstetric source/fetal considerations require clinician review
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Pregnancy or postpartum status changes fever/sepsis evaluation, antimicrobial selection, source control, fetal considerations, and disposition; use maternal sepsis guidance and obstetric consultation.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Acutely ill adult: screen with NEWS/NEWS2/MEWS/SIRS rather than qSOFA alone; sepsis is a clinical diagnosis, not a single biomarker rule-out
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 recommends NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS, or SIRS over qSOFA as a single sepsis screening tool in hospital; sepsis is a clinical diagnosis and should not be ruled in or ruled out with one biomarker/test.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Possible/probable/definite sepsis or shock: collect blood cultures as soon as possible, ideally before antimicrobials, and measure lactate
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: For adults with possible, probable, or definite sepsis or septic shock, SSC recommends blood cultures as soon as possible and ideally before antimicrobials, and suggests measuring blood lactate.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock: at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in first 3 hours with frequent reassessment
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: SSC 2026 suggests at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours for adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock; use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal weight when BMI >30 kg/m2 and reassess frequently.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L; treatment target MAP 65 mm Hg, or 60-65 mm Hg initially if age >=65
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation. SSC 2026 recommends initial MAP target 65 mm Hg in adult septic shock, suggests 60-65 mm Hg in age 65 years or older, and suggests starting vasopressors peripherally rather than delaying for central venous access.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Vasopressor administration and mean arterial pressure

synthetic_patient_scenarios.expected_active_branch: Shock/hypoperfusion: MAP below target, severe hypotension, lactate >=4 mmol/L, lactate >2 mmol/L with vasopressor need, mottling, altered mentation, hypoxemia, oliguria, or rapid deterioration

Low infection likelihood or alternate diagnosis: drug fever, inflammatory/autoimmune flare, thrombosis/PE, malignancy, transfusion reaction, endocrine crisis, or localized nonseptic syndrome better explains findings

Pregnancy/postpartum status, immunocompromise/neutropenia, MDR colonization/prior infection, renal/hepatic dysfunction, antimicrobial allergy, device infection, or institutional protocol changes antimicrobials, source control, dosing, monitoring, or care location
After initial therapy: MAP remains below target, lactate remains elevated, capillary refill/perfusion abnormal, escalating oxygen or vasopressor need, or source control not achieved
Cultures/susceptibility or final negative cultures allow narrowing; adequate source control achieved; or alternate diagnosis demonstrated/strongly suspected
Stable for discharge or lower acuity: source controlled or owned, hemodynamics/oxygenation stable, antimicrobial plan reconciled, pending results assigned, and sepsis education/follow-up documented
Probable/definite infection with organ dysfunction or high-risk trajectory but no current shock: start antimicrobials ideally within 1 hour and define source control need

clinical_cutoff_criteria.label: Adult septic shock physiology: vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg with lactate >2 mmol/L after adequate fluids
clinical_cutoff_criteria.criteria_text: Route suspected infection to septic shock/monitored-care escalation when vasopressors are needed to maintain MAP >=65 mm Hg and lactate remains >2 mmol/L after adequate fluid resuscitation.
clinical_cutoff_criteria.source_section: Adult sepsis and septic shock hemodynamic targets

clinical_cutoff_criteria.label: Adult sepsis resuscitation: lactate >=4 mmol/L or sepsis-induced hypoperfusion supports immediate resuscitation; initial crystalloid often 30 mL/kg
clinical_cutoff_criteria.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis with hypoperfusion or markedly elevated lactate, use immediate resuscitation and reassessment; SSC materials retain 30 mL/kg crystalloid as an initial reference volume for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion/septic shock.
